nativeRange.habitat: Marine benthic habitats on tropical reef flats, rubble zones, and shallow subtidal substrate; tolerates a wide range of hydrological conditions and grows both anchored to hard substrate and epiphytically on other algae or seagrass.

summary: Acanthophora Spiny Red Seaweed (Acanthophora spicifera) is a stiff, branching red alga with fine, spike-like lateral spinelets that give it a distinctly prickly appearance. It is unrelated to the similarly named Eucheuma species, which belong to a different algal order entirely. Color forms range from reddish-purple to reddish-orange.
careNotes:
GENUS NOTE: Acanthophora spicifera belongs to family Rhodomelaceae, order Ceramiales — taxonomically and morphologically distinct from the similarly named Eucheuma species ([sw-algae-092] and [sw-algae-050]), which belong to family Solieriaceae, order Gigartinales. Despite sharing the 'spiny seaweed' trade name, the two genera are not closely related. Acanthophora is recognizable by its finer, more brittle thallus with true spike-shaped lateral spinelets along the main axes, whereas Eucheuma is a stouter, fleshier alga used commercially to produce carrageenan. The species is sold under two color names — Purple Prickly Seaweed (reddish-purple) and Pink Prickly Seaweed (reddish-orange, sometimes labeled Acanthophora sp.) — both of which share the same care requirements.

As a red alga, Acanthophora MUST NOT be placed in the sand bed. Only green algae such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea tolerate sand-bed placement; burying the base of a red or brown alga causes it to melt. Attach to live rock by wedging between rocks (the least stressful method), or secure with a zip-tie, fishing line, or by fragmenting onto a rubble plug.

The thallus is brittle and sensitive to physical stress. Because of this fragility, the species requires express shipping to arrive in good condition — standard ground shipping is generally not suitable.

Lighting: full-spectrum LED is preferred; actinic-only spectrum is generally inadequate for sustained growth. Flow: gentle flow with surface agitation, equivalent to a soft-coral-style setup — excessive direct flow tends to tear the brittle thallus. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and trace micronutrients is generally advised. Target water chemistry: temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm.

Herbivorous tankmates — including tangs, rabbitfish, large angelfish, and sea urchins — tend to consume this alga readily; Acanthophora is documented as a preferred food of herbivorous reef fishes and green sea turtles in the wild.

INVASIVE WARNING: A. spicifera is highly invasive in Hawaii, where it was introduced from Guam to Pearl Harbor in 1950 and is now considered the most common nonindigenous alga in the main Hawaiian Islands. The species regrows from very small tissue fragments. Aquarists in coastal U.S. jurisdictions should never release fragments or water containing this alga into natural waterways or coastal areas.
breedingNotes: Reproduces both sexually (gametophyte/sporophyte alternation) and vegetatively. In aquaria, propagation is by fragmentation — detaching healthy branch segments and securing them to rubble, plug, or rock. The species has been reported to regrow from very small tissue fragments, which contributes to its invasive potential in the wild.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary hobby trade source. Confirmed species identity as Acanthophora spicifera, common name 'Purple Prickly Seaweed,' reddish-purple coloration, stiff/prickly/brittle texture, branching morphology, 2–3" and 5"+ cutting sizes, and shipping sensitivity (UPS 2-Day Express mandatory for live arrival guarantee). AlgaeBarn does not currently carry Acanthophora — the originally-designated primary returned zero search results.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care Guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary. Confirmed target water chemistry (temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm), red-algae sand-bed melt warning, propagation methods (wedging preferred, zip-tie/fishing line acceptable; NO sand bed for red algae), full-spectrum LED lighting requirement, soft-coral-equivalent flow, and weekly iron/iodine/trace micronutrient dosing.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Pink Prickly Seaweed listing
additional.notes: Consolidated sibling listing (Acanthophora sp., reddish-orange 'Pink' color form). Same care profile and shipping sensitivity as the purple form; merged into this single species entry per the one-entry-per-species rule.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Acanthophora spicifera
additional.notes: Taxonomic and ecological reference. Confirmed family Rhodomelaceae, order Ceramiales, class Florideophyceae, division Rhodophyta; native range Guam, Western Australia, and introduced/invasive in Hawaii (Pearl Harbor 1950); reproduction by both sexual cycle and fragmentation; documented as preferred food of herbivorous fishes and green sea turtles.

additional.name: WoRMS — Acanthophora spicifera (M.Vahl) Børgesen, 1910
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority confirmation. Accepted name Acanthophora spicifera (M.Vahl) Børgesen, 1910; WoRMS AphiaID 211768. AlgaeBase entry returned CAPTCHA wall — WoRMS used as taxonomic fallback.
